title: Love Makes Robots Human: Loveability and the Politics of Liveliness
author: Moran

opening_move.description: The paper opens with the puzzle of why some people advocate for the 'rights' of non-living robots while many humans and animals remain killable — diagnosing this as a misallocation of moral concern produced by normative loveability within Western coloniality.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Failing to understand loveability's role in the hierarchy of liveliness risks politically misdirecting empathy toward commodities and away from living beings who are actually dispossessed.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper argues against relational framings of robot rights (Gunkel, Coeckelbergh, Darling) and sex robot advocacy by showing how these reproduce colonial love-plots; it proposes instead a decolonial reading of narrative-encoding via Wynter's autopoiesis framework.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Genealogical narrative analysis (Wynter's autopoiesis as method); close reading of three robots — Samantha (Tropic Gynoid), Kismet (sociable robot), BINA48 (transhumanist cyborg) — as sites of narrative-encoded loveability; cultural history of robot design decisions traced to colonial love-plots.
epistemic_mode.notes: The three robots are interpretive objects, not empirical case studies: the analysis reads the narratives encoding them rather than studying user behaviour or design processes empirically. The argument is normative throughout — concluding that we must resist misdirected empathy toward loveable non-living commodities.

case_type.case_name: Samantha (sex doll AI), Kismet (MIT sociable robot), BINA48 (Terasem transhumanist robot)

normative_stake.domain: justice / colonial politics of life / tech ethics

confidence_notes: The three-robot structure makes the move sequence unusually long and repetitive — each robot constitutes its own case_introduction/development/interpretation cycle. The rival_mapping and immanent_critique come late (in the robot rights section), making this an unusual variant where case analysis precedes rival engagement. Epistemic mode is clearly normative_with_interpretive_case: the robots are read as cultural texts/symptoms, not studied as empirical objects. The normative stake is high and explicit: the conclusion calls for resistance to misdirected moral concern.
